intro: The USDA Plant Hardiness Zone Map helps gardeners choose plants that will thrive in their climate. Each zone is based on the average minimum winter temperature in a given area. By knowing your hardiness zone, you can select ornamental plants, trees, and shrubs that will survive your coldest winters. This calculator determines your USDA hardiness zone based on your region's minimum winter temperature. Data updated to 2026.
keyTakeaway: Formula: **Official USDA Plant Hardiness Zone Table**. Enter your minimum winter temperature to instantly find your hardiness zone and discover which plants will survive your climate.

fields.label: Minimum Winter Temperature (°C)
fields.help: The average minimum temperature during winter in your area

explanation:
## Understanding USDA Hardiness Zones

The USDA Plant Hardiness Zone Map divides North America into 13 zones (1–13) based on average minimum winter temperatures. Each zone spans 10°F (5.5°C), and zones are subdivided into 'a' and 'b' regions representing 5°F (2.8°C) increments.

Knowing your hardiness zone helps you:
- Select plants that will survive your winters
- Plan multi-year landscaping projects
- Understand plant labels and growing guides
- Avoid purchasing plants that won't thrive

## How to Use This Calculator

Enter your region's average minimum winter temperature in Celsius. The calculator will return your USDA hardiness zone and a summary. Use this zone when shopping for plants, reading gardening guides, or planning your landscape.

## Important Notes

This calculator provides an estimate based on temperature alone. Actual plant hardiness depends on factors like soil quality, drainage, sunlight exposure, wind protection, humidity, and your specific microclimate. For critical landscaping decisions, consult a local nursery or horticultural extension office. Data is current as of 2026.

faq.q: What is a USDA hardiness zone?
faq.a: A USDA hardiness zone is a geographical designation based on average annual minimum winter temperatures. It helps gardeners determine which plants can survive outdoors year-round in their climate.

faq.q: How do I use my hardiness zone when buying plants?
faq.a: Check the plant tag or description for a zone recommendation (e.g., 'Hardy to Zone 7'). This means the plant should survive winter in zones 7 and warmer. Match it to your zone or choose plants rated for lower zones to be safe.

faq.q: Can I grow plants from warmer zones in my area?
faq.a: Not reliably outdoors year-round. Plants rated for warmer zones will likely die during your coldest winter. You can grow them in containers and bring them indoors, or look for cold-hardy alternatives.
Zone 7a represents areas where the average minimum winter temperature is –17.8°C to –15°C. The 'a' and 'b' subdivisions represent 2.8°C differences within each zone.

faq.q: Do hardiness zones account for all growing conditions?
faq.a: No. Zones are based on temperature only. Actual plant survival also depends on soil, drainage, humidity, sunlight, and wind protection. Your specific garden may be warmer or colder than the general zone.

Yes, it uses the official USDA Plant Hardiness Zone methodology. However, microclimates (areas near walls, water, or protected spots) can vary by 1–2 zones. Always verify with local experts for critical plantings.
Yes, all our calculators are completely free and require no registration.
No. All calculations run in your browser. We don't send your information to any server.

faq.q: What if my garden has multiple zones?
faq.a: Many gardens have microclimates. South-facing walls, sheltered areas, or spots near buildings can be 1–2 zones warmer. Low spots where cold air settles may be colder. Choose plants based on your coldest spot, or group plantings by microclimate.

sources.name: NIST — National Institute of Standards and Technology

dataUpdate.notes: USDA zone formula is fixed and does not require external data updates
